1: Zechariah prophesied ca. 520-518 BCE (Zechariah 1-8, with the dated oracles) and possibly into the 5th or 4th century BCE (Zechariah 9-14, the 'Second Zechariah', are undated and stylistically different — many scholars treat them as later additions). His eight night visions (Zechariah 1-6) address the post-exilic restoration community with complex symbolic imagery; his oracle-collections (Zechariah 7-8, 9-11, 12-14) look further into the eschatological future. Zechariah is the most extensively quoted OT book in the Gospel passion narratives — his oracles about the triumphal entry (Zechariah 9:9), the thirty pieces of silver (Zechariah 11:12-13), the smitten shepherd (Zechariah 13:7), the pierced one (Zechariah 12:10), and the cosmic mourning (Zechariah 12:10-14) all find explicit NT citations in the passion story. The passion narrative is Zechariah 9-14 in fulfillment.
2: 'YHWH was very angry with your fathers.' The opening word of the prophetic commission is not vision but history: YHWH's prior anger with the pre-exilic generation. This historical grounding is essential — the visions that follow are not free-floating apocalyptic but specific responses to Israel's particular covenant history. The anger is acknowledged, not minimized, as the context for the restoration promise that immediately follows.
3: 'Therefore say to them: Thus declares YHWH of hosts: Return to me, says YHWH of hosts, and I will return to you, says YHWH of hosts.' The double-return formula (shuvu elai... ve'ashuv aleichem) is the condensed covenant-restoration theology of Deuteronomy 30:2-3 and the entire prophetic tradition. YHWH's return to his people is conditional on their return to him — but the conditionality here is gracious rather than coercive: the invitation comes before any evidence of repentance.
4: 'Do not be like your fathers, to whom the former prophets cried out, Thus says YHWH of hosts, Return from your evil ways and from your evil deeds. But they did not hear or pay attention to me, declares YHWH.' The negative exemplar: the pre-exilic generation who heard the prophets (Amos, Hosea, Isaiah, Micah, Jeremiah) and refused to return. The exile was the result of that refusal. The post-exilic generation is called not to repeat the pattern. The reminder that the former prophets said the same thing connects Zechariah to the whole prophetic tradition as its culminating and fulfilling voice.
5: 'Your fathers, where are they? And the prophets, do they live forever?' The rhetorical question: the fathers who refused to return are dead; the prophets who warned them are dead. But the word they spoke has outlasted them both and proved true. This is Zechariah's implicit claim for prophetic authority: history has vindicated the former prophets — their predictions came true. Now listen to this prophet while there is time.
6: 'But my words and my statutes, which I commanded my servants the prophets, did they not overtake your fathers? So they repented and said, As YHWH of hosts purposed to deal with us for our ways and deeds, so has he dealt with us.' The word of YHWH overtook the fathers like pursuing judgment (hissigu — the same root used of the curse that overtakes/catches the disobedient in Deuteronomy 28:15, 45). The exile confirmed the prophetic word. The post-exilic generation itself acknowledged this: the exile was just.
7: 'On the twenty-fourth day of the eleventh month, which is the month of Shebat, in the second year of Darius, the word of YHWH came to the prophet Zechariah, the son of Berechiah, son of Iddo.' The date of the night visions: 15 February 519 BCE — five months after the opening call (Zechariah 1:1) and two months after Haggai's final oracle (Haggai 2:20 = ninth month, twenty-fourth day = 18 December 520 BCE). The night visions form a literary unit framed by this date formula.
8: 'I saw in the night, and behold, a man riding on a red horse! He was standing among the myrtle trees in the glen, and behind him were red, sorrel, and white horses.' The first night vision: a man on a red horse standing in the myrtle grove. The myrtle (hadassim) is a fragrant shrub that grows in ravines — a scene of hidden beauty and peace in a low place, contrasting with the grandeur of the expected divine dwelling on high mountains. The colored horses (red, reddish-brown, white) will recur in the fourth vision (Zechariah 6) as the four chariots, suggesting a systematic cosmic patrol.
9: 'Then I said, What are these, my lord? The angel who talked with me said to me, I will show you what they are.' The interpreting angel (malach hammedabber bi, the angel speaking with me) is Zechariah's guide throughout the night visions — a feature distinctive from earlier prophetic vision reports (cf. Amos, Isaiah, Jeremiah) but paralleled in Ezekiel (40-48) and the apocalyptic literature (Daniel 8-12). The angel mediates understanding, indicating that the visions are not self-interpreting.
10: 'So the man who was standing among the myrtle trees answered and said, These are they whom YHWH has sent to patrol the earth.' The cosmic patrol: YHWH's angelic agents survey the entire earth and report back. The political intelligence gathered by these agents will be reported in Zechariah 1:11. The patrol concept reflects the divine-council theology of Job 1-2 and 1 Kings 22:19-22 — YHWH has agents in the world reporting on its condition.
11: 'And they answered the angel of YHWH who was standing among the myrtle trees, and said, We have patrolled the earth, and behold, all the earth remains at rest.' The report: the whole earth is at rest (yoshevet veshaqetet, sitting and at peace). This is politically disturbing: Haggai had prophesied the shaking of the nations (Haggai 2:6-7, 21-22), which the post-exilic community expected to inaugurate the Davidic restoration. But the nations are at peace — Darius's empire is stable. YHWH's people are suffering while the nations prosper. The vision addresses this theodicy directly.
12: 'Then the angel of YHWH said, O YHWH of hosts, how long will you have no mercy on Jerusalem and the cities of Judah, against which you have been angry these seventy years?' The angel of YHWH intercedes for Jerusalem — how long until the exile-judgment is reversed? The seventy years echoes Jeremiah's prediction (Jeremiah 25:11-12; Zechariah 29:10) and Daniel's reflection on it (Daniel 9:2). From 586 BCE (Jerusalem's fall) to 519 BCE (this vision) is approximately 67 years — close to seventy. The question is not whether YHWH will restore but when.
13: 'And YHWH answered gracious and comforting words to the angel who talked with me.' The answer is given first as a characterization: gracious (tovim) and comforting (menachamim) words — the divine comfort that Nahum's name announced, that Zephaniah 3:17 promised (YHWH rejoicing over his people), now spoken directly to the waiting angel. The content follows in Zechariah 1:14-17.
14: 'So the angel who talked with me said to me, Cry out, Thus says YHWH of hosts: I am exceedingly jealous for Jerusalem and for Zion.' The divine jealousy (qine'ti lirushalaym veletzion qin'ah gedolah) is the same covenant-passion language of Exodus 20:5 — YHWH's exclusive and protective possessiveness over his covenant people. The jealousy here is not punitive (as in Nahum 1:2) but restorative: YHWH's jealousy for Jerusalem is the motivation for her restoration.
15: 'And I am exceedingly angry with the nations that are at ease; for while I was angry but a little, they furthered the disaster.' The nations' complacency is YHWH's grievance against them — they were tools of his judgment but exceeded their mandate. They were instruments of limited punishment who made the disaster worse than YHWH intended. This is the principle behind Isaiah 10:5-7 (Assyria as YHWH's rod who exceeded his authority) and Jeremiah 50:33-34 (Babylon who held Israel prisoner beyond the appointed time).
16: 'Therefore, thus says YHWH, I have returned to Jerusalem with mercy; my house shall be built in it, declares YHWH of hosts, and the measuring line shall be stretched out over Jerusalem.' The three promises: YHWH's return to Jerusalem, the temple's rebuilding (the primary concern of Haggai and Zechariah), and the measuring line stretched out — the surveyor's line that marks the city's planned expansion. The measuring line will appear again in Zechariah 2:1 (the second vision) as a measuring of Jerusalem's coming extent.
17: 'Cry out again, Thus says YHWH of hosts: My cities shall again overflow with prosperity, and YHWH will again comfort Zion and again choose Jerusalem.' The restoration of scattered cities and the double comfort of Zion. The verb 'overflow' (yafutzu od) — abundance spilling over the container's capacity. YHWH's re-choice of Jerusalem (vebachar od beYerushalaim) declares that the election is not revoked — the exile did not cancel YHWH's choice of Zion as his dwelling place.
18: 'And I lifted my eyes and saw, and behold, four horns!' The second night vision: four horns (qarnot). Horns were the symbol of power throughout the ancient Near East — royal power, military strength, divine might (cf. Psalms 75:5-6; Daniel 7:7-8; Zechariah 8:3). The four horns represent the four major world-empires that had scattered Judah, Israel, and Jerusalem. The number four signals totality — all directions of the compass, all the forces that had oppressed the covenant people.
19: 'And I said to the angel who talked with me, What are these? And he said to me, These are the horns that have scattered Judah, Israel, and Jerusalem.' The interpreting angel identifies the horns as the powers that scattered (zeruh) the covenant people — Assyria, Babylon, and the subsequent empires. The inclusion of both Judah and Israel signals that the oracle encompasses the whole twelve-tribe people, not just the Babylonian exiles who returned.
20: 'Then YHWH showed me four craftsmen (charashim). And I said, What are these coming to do? He said, These are the horns that scattered Judah, so that no one raised his head. And these have come to terrify them, to cast down the horns of the nations who lifted up their horn against the land of Judah to scatter it.' The craftsmen/smiths (charashim — metal-workers or carpenters) come to terrify (lechared) and cast down the horns. Each oppressive power is balanced by a craftsman-power that will dismantle it. The vision answers the theodicy of Zechariah 1:11 (the nations at ease): they are not permanently safe; YHWH has sent agents to terrify them.
21: 'Then YHWH showed me four craftsmen.' This verse (in Hebrew Zechariah 2:1) begins the explicit description of the craftsmen's work: they have come to terrify the horn-nations and cast them down. The vision of four horns balanced by four craftsmen establishes a theological principle: every power that oppresses YHWH's people will meet a counter-power that dismantles it. No empire is permanent; YHWH holds the balance of world history.
